There (most often pronounced /ðə(r)/) is used as an introductory subject in 2  introductory         subject «81.

For the difference between here and there, ► There's the book I was looking for.

What's that green thing over there?

There (pronounced /ðeə(r)/) is an adverb meaning 'in that place'.

1  adverb      of  place pronunciations and uses.

The  spelling there is used for two words with completely different 605 there Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 607 vocabulary' •         through: time The park is open from May through September.

In American English, through can be used to mean 'up to and including'.

For I (don't) think so and I thought so, I should have thought he could have washed his hands, at least.

This structure can also introduce criticisms.

I'd have thought we could expect at least forty people.

I was wondering if I could do anything to help. (More natural than I was Think does not usually introduce indirect questions.

6 indirect      speech Hello! I thought you weren't coming!

However, we  can express surprise with I thought... not.

Emily doesn't think she can come.

J don't think it will rain. (More natural than I think it won't rain.) think, rather than with the following clause (►   219).

When  think is used to introduce a negative clause, we  most often put not with 5  transferred        negation:      I don't     think      . . .

He was thought to be a spy.

reasonably common, usually with to be.

object           + complement However, the passive equivalent of the                                   structure is We thought that he was a fool.

They thought that she was fascinating.

In more normal styles, /^/-clauses are preferred after think.

They thought him  to be a spy.

judgement rather than subjective impression), but this is very unusual.

To be is occasionally used before the complement (suggesting objective vocabulary' •  608  time For similar structures with other time words, and with place, way and reason, ►     237.7.                              -4 The first time (that) I saw her, my heart stopped.

style (or dropped).

In relative structures after time, that is often used instead of when in an informal You won 't fool me this time.
